text.heading: Aliyu vs Kure: A battle IBB could not settle

p.italics: Despite the intervention of two former Heads of State, Ibrahim Babangida and Abdulsalami Abubakar, Niger State Governor Babangida Aliyu and his predecessor, Abdulkadir Kure sink deeper into the trenches, writes OLUKOREDE YISHAU

text.p: Abdulkadir Kure was governor of Niger State from May 29, 1999 to May 29, 2007. He was succeeded by Babangida Alyu, who is also a member of the ruling People’s Democratic Party (PDP). Kure’s wife, who is a Senator, and his six children may soon have to deal with a situation where their father is picked up by the police over an indictment for corruption. The days when he had immunity from arrest and prosecution ended May 29, 2007.

Unlike Kure, who was addressed as His Excellency, Aliyu dropped the fanciful title for the less glamorous title of Chief Servant. And as the Chief Servant, he says the people of the Power State are his boss, and on their behalf he would fight anyone.

Since he mounted the saddle as the governor, Aliyu has been fighting his predecessor, who claims he is just being vilified for nothing. But the governor counters by saying he is all out for the people. A probe instituted by Aliyu into the activities of his predecessor has led to a white paper banning Kure from running for public office for ten years.

Five other politicians have also been banned from holding public office in the next ten years. They include the former Vice Chairman of the PDP for the North Central Zone, Alhaji Abubakar Magaji and the former Commissioner for Justice and Attorney General in the state, Alhaji Umar Kawu, former Permanent Secretary of the state’s Ministry of Local Government and Chieftaincy Affairs, Alhaji Alhassan Jibrin Bala, and a former director in the ministry, Alhaji Mohammed Katuka. They were all key members of the Kure administration.

Kure, who is now Chairman of the Federal Emergency Road Maintenance Agency (FERMA), had tried to stop the Judicial Commission of Inquiry established by Aliyu last year. The court refused to grant his prayers seeking to stop the inquiry. The seven man commission was set up to probe malpractices relating to the awards and execution of contracts under the immediate past administration between 1999 and 2007.

Kure was indicted over the supply of 200metric tones of iron rod, and ordered to account for N99million the state loss to wrong computation. The white paper also indicted the former governor in the supply of ductile iron pipe fittings and water pumps to Lapai Agaie, Katcha, Kontagora, New Bussa, Lambata, Mokwa and Gawu Babangida by BEEDASH Nigeria Limited under the multi million Naira state-wide water scheme. The former governor was also directed by the government to account for N708million, for which he may face prosecution. The White paper also found Alhassan and Katuka wanting of financial malpractices in their handling of the state/local government joint account, while Kawu was indicted over his handling of the state tender board as the chairman of the board. It also indicted the new Speaker of the Niger State House of Assembly, Alhaji Saidu Ndako Idris, who was asked to refund N89 million allegedly misappropriated during the last administration when he was the Chairman of Mokwa Local Government Area of the state. He is also asked to refund over N97 million to the government coffers being the amount illegally withdrawn from the local government joint account and the treasury of the Mokwa Local Government Council.

As a result of this, the Niger State Police Command has declared him and eight others wanted. The state Police Public Relations Officer, Richard Oguche (ASP), confirmed that a warrant of arrest had been issued against the speaker and eight others.
Oguche said: "The police had no choice than to take the action after the affected persons had failed to voluntarily hand themselves over to the police after summons were served on them."

He said the police took the action because of a report from the Maj-Gen Mamman Kontagora Implementation Committee. "We have not seen them up till now but I can assure you that we will get them to ensure they face prosecution," Oguche said. For Kure, these are indeed trying times. Keen observers of events in the state had predicted that the ex-governor would find it difficult to come out clean after the enquiry. In March 2008, Aliyu alleged that Kure awarded contracts verbally, without documentations. He said his administration inherited N23 billion debt from the Kure administration, and was able to verify only N13 billion of it. This was about the same time the commission ordered the arrest of the managing director of a Jos-based contracting firm, Rishabel Interior, for failure to honour its summons. Alhaji Babangida Suleiman was summoned to appear before the panel twice to give evidence regarding a N10.5 million contract given to the company. The contract was for the supply of 1,000 double bunk beds to Niger State Ministry of Education. The contract was awarded in 2000 of which N8 million was paid as part payment. The contract was among the failed contracts investigated by the panel. Testifying before the panel, Kure’s Commissioner for Education, Alhaji Abubakar Daja said the contractor did not execute the contract.

Significantly, two former Heads of State from the state, Generals Ibrahim Babangida and Abdulsalami Abubakar, are said to have waded into the crisis between the duo, with the hope of finding a solution. At that time, it was assumed that a truce was recorded.

Now, it is clear to all that whatever truce was reached has broken down. And depending on the way the Kontagora Committee chooses to go about it, Kure may be arrested. Others indicted by the commission are being sought by the police already.
The last has certainly not been heard of the drama in Niger. It is just unfolding.
